dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a diabetic emergency. Male in his 40s found unresponsive next to his car in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMT. Can you tell me what's happened here?
He was eating, took insulin, then passed out about 30 minutes ago.
Open your eyes for me, sir. Can you hear me?
No response. Confirming for breathing now.
Airway is patent and not obstructed. Nothing needed for now.
Breathing is within normal limits. Readings are at 11 with no signs of distress.
Respirations are 11, not labored, and even.
Pulse is 81, regular and strong with no irregularities noted.
Blood pressure is 159/83, stable at this point.
Skin is cool and clammy, but no trauma or bleeding. Pulse is strong bilaterally.
Glucose is 34 mg/dL. That is very low-hypoglycemia confirmed.
4-lead EKG is in place and showing sinus rhythm. No acute changes present.
Pupils are 4mm, equal, and reactive to light and tracking well. Normocephalic with no swelling or deformity. No signs of trauma or lymphadenopathy in neck area.
Equal bilateral chest movement. Breath sounds are clear (BBS), no wheezes or crackles. Heart sounds normal on auscultation.
Strong peripheral pulses. No swelling, deformities, or DCAP-BTLS noted in arms or legs. Normal muscle tone and control. Back appears intact with no DCAP-BTLS reported.
Can anyone here tell me what he was doing right before the event occurred?
He had a snack around 6:00 PM and then took his regular dose of 40U insulin before collapsing about 30 minutes after.
Does he have any medical conditions or medications his family knows about we should be aware of?
Yes, he has type 1 diabetes and his blood pressure is also high. On a few medications including Lantus, metformin, and some blood pressure meds like losartan and chlorthalidone.
Based on current findings, he's in protocol 3-8: medical-diabetic-hypoglycemia. Partner-get 10% dextrose ready for IV now.
IV placed in left antecubital with 18G catheter. Administering 250 mL of 10% dextrose now per protocol.
After glucose, heart rate is still 81. Respirations improved to 18-still within normal limits.
He opens eyes to voice, answers appropriately, and localizes pain. GCS from 3 to 15 with improvement observed post-glucose. A/Ox4/4.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how would you rate any discomfort you might be facing now, sir?
Not any pain-feels fine. Maybe a 0 currently if anything at all.
Can you guide me again through what happened before the event?
I had a snack at around 6:00, then took my regular insulin shot. I started feeling odd after that and just blacked out. No other symptoms I noticed again now after waking up.
79 for heart rate, 18 for respirations. Blood pressure 146/86. SpO₂ 98%. Still stable.
Chest movement is still adequate. BBS are clear in both lungs. No new issues in torso or limbs observed. No DCAP-BTLS reported on second exam.
Mr., do you want to come with us to the hospital to make sure everything is clear before you leave the scene or would you prefer to go home now after being alert and tested okay?
I feel fine. Just a bit shaken, but I am good to stay home and rest up from this. Thanks for helping me back up, though.
Mr. has been made aware of the choice in refusal and has signed the paperwork. No planned movement at this time. Our care is fully documented and will remain so for legal backup purposes.
Partner, can you confirm again on the eye responses for documentation?
Pupils confirmed as PERRL and he is fully oriented. Responds well and seems mentally clear again. No DCAP-BTLS observed during head-to-toe reassessment.
The patient is semi-Fowler positioned with belt secured. Continuously monitored. Declines transport after symptom return and is noted as alert in reassessment.
All vital signs normal and stable. Heart rate 79 now, 18 breaths a minute, 146/86 recorded for BP and SpO₂ at 98%. Fully sinus rhythm consistent with last assessment data.

Protocol 3-8 fully followed with worsening noted during initial hypoglycemia and improved after dextrose IV. Now vital signs stable, with resolved symptoms observed and the patient is alert and oriented.
All steps per protocol are documented as completed successfully with patient improved and condition stable for refusal decision without hospital transport likely needed.
All readings consistent with prior reads-heart rate 79, BP remains steady at 146/86, SpO₂ 98%. Relatively low rate likely due to glucose correction effect now.
Glucose is within normal range now. No additional care plans are required per protocol. All findings meet criteria for patient refusal at this stage.
We have ensured patient understands risks of refusal, and he has signed the appropriate forms to clarify his unwillingness to be taken to the hospital for further evaluation at the time.
Patient is fully aware cognitively now, reassured with normalized readings shown, with no symptoms of any pain at this time, and willing to undergo final offline refusal discussion per regulations.
All required responsibilities and documentation have been completed as per written protocol 3-8 and considered enforceable within legal medical ethics for transport refusal scenarios.
Thank you, I'm very aware of my choices. I have decided to not go to the hospital today and manage this. My health should be okay in view of my upcoming appointment this week.
Will you see a doctor soon to get further checkup done over this episode, sir?
My doctor did call me back last week about my glucose levels and all. I was supposed to go for a check up just during this week. So I will make sure I keep that appointment at the earliest.
EMT documentation includes action plan for follow-up, family check-ins, and monitoring at the patient's home environment proactively.
Family is present on site and willingly assured to support the patient from home. Will keep symptoms monitored for any sign of recurrence at home going forward.
The patient has been safely returned to family care under our supervision. No medical issues arose during the entire assessment and treatment process.
